docs.title: Welcome to the Snapmaker U1 unofficial Community Wiki
docs.text: This site is still under construction. Please refer to the Discord for more info.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
You are welcome to contribute!

To do so, please create a Pull Request (PR) for this repo. Guidance on how to create PR can be found in the official github guide. You don't even need to download any files. Make a fork, edit the files with the github web-based text editor, and submit a PR.

Please attribute the original author and link to the initial source, where possible. Don't overthink your contributions too much. "Good enough" is good enough. Better badly documented than not at all.

docs.title: Official Firmware
docs.text: Find the documentation and releases of the official firmware in the official wiki.

docs.title: Custom Firmware
docs.text: a.k.a. paxx12 firmware, more info here.

docs.title: Custom Firmware
docs.text: You can install a custom firmware on your U1 to enable more features. Find the documentation here.

docs.title: Warning!
docs.text:
While installing custom firmware does not automatically void the product warranty, any damage caused by or attributable to the installation or use of custom firmware is not covered under warranty. Use at your own risk. See Snapmaker Terms of Use for details.

Custom firmware is intended for users with appropriate technical knowledge. Ensure you understand the implications before proceeding.

docs.title: Cooling
docs.text: • Cheecky's Driver Heatsink Mod relocates the driver heatsink to the backside of the mainboard and adds a cooling fan.

docs.title: Slicer
docs.text: • Full Spectrum slicer to print more than 4 colors with just 4 filaments (Discord link), Github Link

docs.title: Common issues with the printer
docs.text: • Clog errors and creaking with PETG

docs.title: Common issues with prints
docs.text: • Pitting, holes, and pockmarks in prints

docs.title: Feeder creaking or making noises, but feeding fine
Description
docs.text: When filament is passing through the feeder, one or both filament paths creak.

docs.title: Cause
docs.text: Filament gets rolled through the feeder, but the rollers inside are plastic on plastic without any lubrification.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: Dissassemble the feeder and grease/lubricate the rollers.

docs.title: Creaking and clog errors when printing PETG
Description
docs.text: On a stock U1, with the spools loaded on the side and the feeders installed like the manual instructs, printing with PETG can lead to clog detection and cause creaking.

docs.title: Cause
docs.text: The PETG rubs against the metal inlet rings on the feeders, especially the bottom spools, causing them to creak and eventually to underextrude and raise a clog detection error.

docs.title: Solutions
docs.text: Print this PTFE adapter for the feeders and insert a few cm/inches of PTFE. I recommend using M10 or ECAS04 couplings, as the M6 coupling doesn't allow the tube to go through the coupling and "connect" to the feeder inlet ring. Alternatively, one can also print these TPU PTFE adapters.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The print has irregular holes in the vertical surfaces (in layer lines) and they're not related to seams.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: Credit: reappear.

docs.title: Official SnapMaker Wiki
docs.text: SnapMaker has a guide in their official Wiki. TL;DR Reduce or tune retraction.

docs.title: Print profiles
docs.text: Better PLA print profiles that favor quality over speed can be found in this Discord thread.

docs.title: Guides
docs.text: • Dissassembling the feeders and greasing the filament rollers

docs.title: Dissassembling the feeders and greasing the filament rollers
1) Unscrew the two screws with a 2mm allen key
2) Loosen the bottom screw
docs.text: You can unscrew it completely or just loosen it a few millimeters. If you unscrew it completely, it might fall into the bottom shell and get stuck on the motor (it's magnetic). It's not an issue, but be aware of it.

docs.title: 3) Get the feeder out of the bottom shell
docs.text: There are tabs that you need to undo. Then you can lift the feeder out to the side from under the fixed tabs. You may need to lift sideways in a few directions to get around the bottom screw from step 2.

docs.title: 4) Disconnect and remove feeeder PCB
docs.text: Flip the little latch up carefully. Use a flat spudger if you are afraid to do it with your finger nails. The flat cable should then be loose and able to be taken out. Then unscrew the single top screw completely and loosen the other two bottom screw. BE CAREFUL WHEN SLIDING OUT THE PCB! The magnets and springs are held in place by the PCB and might jump out and get lost! It is advised to take out the springs and magnets and store them. Store the magnets separate from any other parts, especially the springs!

docs.title: This is still a WIP, but the above steps should get you there 90%
